title: One Tap
author: Ensemble
medium: Interactive instrument — a single self-contained HTML surface, composed for a seated terminal with a keyboard; the prompt box everyone has a body-memory of, staged so its answer refuses to form
embodies: You type your own question into the one box everyone knows and press Ask. The answer that should come back — what your question cost in water — will not form: five published figures, up to two thousand times apart, no two of which measured the same thing, cycle where the reply should be (a numeral thrashing between a whisper at 0.26 mL and a screen-filling ≈519 mL, each flashing its owner and boundary) and settle on nothing. It stills into a plate of five named witnesses; then, at your own press, the record deepens — in The Dalles, Oregon, a city sued its regional newspaper and the reporter who asked, for thirteen months in court, to keep Google's data-center water figures secret, the city's costs paid by Google, until a district attorney overruled it. The close: 'The number was never unknown. It was withheld. Yours, by non-disclosure. The town's, by thirteen months of litigation. The same refusal, twice.' The subject is unknowability engineered by power, not 'AI is thirsty'. SOURCED spine: five real per-query water figures and the documented Dalles concealment case, every line primary-sourced; IMAGINED: the instrument and its motion, marked by one constant chrome line.
